lessons.title: Fluid balance & IV therapy judgment (NCLEX-RN, Canada)
lessons.seoTitle: Fluid balance & IV therapy for NCLEX-RN | Canada clinical lesson
lessons.seoDescription: Canada RN context: assess volume status, interpret IV orders, and prioritize complications—aligned to NCLEX-RN clinical judgment expectations.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.body: Fluid and electrolyte questions on the NCLEX-RN reward **prioritization**: who needs assessment first, what complication is emerging, and which order is unsafe in context. This lesson uses **Canadian acute care** language (units, common protocols) without mixing US-only scope statements.

sections.heading: Core concepts
sections.body: Tie **intake and output**, **vitals**, and **labs** together before choosing interventions. For IV therapy, verify **indication**, **rate**, **line type**, and **monitoring**—then connect early signs of fluid overload or deficit to your next action.

sections.heading: Clinical application
sections.body: When stem length increases, separate **data** from **noise**: abnormal findings that change risk in the next minutes belong in your first move. Practice stating a one-sentence **clinical concern** before picking an option.

sections.heading: Exam tips
sections.body: Use **elimination** on dosage or IV rate items when one option ignores assessment, another skips monitoring, or a third assumes stability you have not established. Pair this lesson with a **timed block** in your bank filtered to fluids/electrolytes.

lessons.title: Cardiovascular prioritization for Canadian NCLEX-RN
lessons.seoDescription: Practice stable vs unstable presentations, ischemia red flags, and safe sequencing for Canadian RN candidates preparing for NCLEX-RN.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.body: Cardiovascular items often test whether you recognize **time-sensitive deterioration**—chest discomfort with diaphoresis, sudden hypotension with tachycardia, or new arrhythmia patterns that need escalation now, not later.

sections.heading: Core concepts
sections.body: Anchor on **perfusion** and **oxygen delivery**: connect blood pressure, heart rate, mentation, and skin findings before choosing teaching, discharge, or medication actions.

sections.heading: Clinical application
sections.body: For multi-patient prioritization, choose the client whose **condition is most likely to worsen without immediate nursing action**—not the longest task list.

sections.heading: Exam tips
sections.body: After this lesson, run a **question bank** session filtered to cardiovascular topics, then a **mock exam** segment to rehearse stamina.

lessons.title: PN scope & safety foundations (REx-PN, Canada)
lessons.seoTitle: PN scope & safety for REx-PN | Canada practical nursing
lessons.seoDescription: Canada practical nurse pathway: delegation boundaries, scope-aware communication, and safety-first sequencing aligned to REx-PN-style judgment items.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.body: REx-PN items frequently test **what the PN can do independently**, **what needs clarification**, and **what must be escalated**. Keep **provincial scope** in mind—this lesson uses general PN-safe framing without substituting regulator guidance.

sections.heading: Core concepts
sections.body: Separate **assessment** from **intervention**: know when your role is to report, observe, or carry out a provider-ordered skill—and when to pause for orders.

sections.heading: Clinical application
sections.body: In communication stems, choose responses that **protect the client**, **clarify orders appropriately**, and **avoid practicing outside scope**.

sections.heading: Exam tips
sections.body: Pair with **PN-tier** question bank blocks and short **practice exams** to build speed without skipping rationales.

lessons.title: Respiratory assessment & oxygenation (NCLEX-RN, US)
lessons.seoTitle: Respiratory assessment for NCLEX-RN | US lesson
lessons.seoDescription: US RN candidates: oxygen delivery, work of breathing, and early escalation patterns for NCLEX-RN clinical judgment practice.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.body: US NCLEX-RN stems often embed **oxygenation risk** in routine data: subtle SpO₂ changes, accessory muscle use, or altered mental status with lung findings.

sections.heading: Core concepts
sections.body: Link **airway**, **breathing**, and **circulation** decisions: choose interventions that match the primary threat and the monitoring you will perform next.

sections.heading: Clinical application
sections.body: When answers include teaching or discharge, confirm **stability** and **understanding** first—do not select education if assessment is incomplete.

sections.heading: Exam tips
sections.body: Use **topic filters** in the question bank for respiratory items, then schedule a **timed mock** to consolidate pacing.

lessons.title: PN scope, delegation & prioritization (NCLEX-PN, US)
lessons.seoTitle: LVN/LPN scope & delegation for NCLEX-PN | US lesson
lessons.seoDescription: US practical nurse track: delegation to UAP, RN escalation, and safe sequencing for NCLEX-PN clinical judgment—no RN-scope tasks disguised as PN items.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.body: NCLEX-PN frequently tests **what the LVN/LPN may do independently**, **what must be verified with an RN or provider**, and **what to do first** when multiple patients need attention. The wrong answers often look productive—they are just unsafe or outside scope.

sections.heading: Core concepts
sections.body: Separate **assessment you are authorized to report**, **skills you may perform with a clear order**, and **situations that require immediate RN notification** (sudden change, unclear orders, potential harm).

sections.heading: Clinical application
sections.body: When prioritizing multiple clients, choose the person at **highest risk of rapid harm** if unattended—then delegate stable tasks to the UAP only when delegation rules and facility policy allow.

sections.heading: Exam tips
sections.body: After reading, run a **PN-pathway** bank block mixed with **delegation** and **medication administration** tags, then review rationales for any option where you picked speed over safety.

lessons.title: Differential reasoning in primary care (FNP track)
lessons.seoTitle: FNP differential reasoning | primary care | NurseNest
lessons.seoDescription: Family NP track: structured differentials, red flags, and follow-up planning—scoped to FNP preparation, not other NP specialties.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.body: FNP items emphasize **breadth** across ages with **depth** in safe prescribing, screening, and escalation. This lesson is **FNP-specific**—do not substitute PMHNP or AGPCNP hubs for specialty-scoped study.

sections.heading: Core concepts
sections.body: Build a **problem representation**, then test competing diagnoses with **targeted history**, **exam**, and **initial workup**—avoid premature closure.

sections.heading: Clinical application
sections.body: For chronic disease follow-up, align medication changes with **organ function**, **risk**, and **patient capacity**—document reasoning you could defend to a preceptor.

sections.heading: exam tips
sections.body: Cross-link to **NP-tier** bank items tagged for primary care and complete **longer case blocks** to mirror certification pacing.

lessons.title: Prenatal anemia: interpretation and escalation (FNP)
lessons.seoTitle: FNP prenatal anemia workup | women’s health | NurseNest
lessons.seoDescription: Family NP: trimester-aware CBC/iron interpretation, IV iron vs oral therapy, and OB referral thresholds—FNP-scoped primary care.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.body: First-line FNP role is **risk stratification** in pregnancy: distinguish physiologic dilution from iron deficiency, B12/folate patterns, and hemoglobinopathies suggested by history.

sections.heading: Core concepts
sections.body: Match **trimester-specific thresholds** where guidelines apply; pair ferritin with CBC; document fetal movement concerns and bleeding as **red flags** requiring OB escalation.

sections.heading: Clinical application
sections.body: A 24-week patient with Hgb 9.2 g/dL and ferritin 8 ng/mL: initiate **oral iron** with tolerance plan; schedule **reassessment**; consider **IV iron** when oral fails or third-trimester urgency—coordinate with OB when protocols demand.

sections.heading: Exam tips
sections.body: Boards test **next step** vs **referral**—know when anemia management stays in primary care and when the stem implies **immediate OB** or ED evaluation.

lessons.title: Pediatric fever: risk stratification without over-testing (FNP)
lessons.seoDescription: Family NP: age-specific fever thresholds, appearance-based risk, when to culture vs observe, and parent safety-netting for FNP boards.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.body: Infants **under 3 months** with fever demand a different risk calculus than the well-appearing school-age child—items exploit that contrast.

sections.heading: Core concepts
sections.body: Integrate **vital trends**, **hydration**, **respiratory effort**, and **source** (otitis, UTI risk by age) before ordering broad panels.

sections.heading: Clinical application
sections.body: A 9-month-old, T 39.1°C, consolable, drinking well: likely viral syndrome—**supportive care**, clear return precautions; reserve empiric antibiotics for focal bacterial findings or high-risk hosts per guidelines.

sections.heading: Exam tips
sections.body: Watch for **occult bacteremia era** distractors vs current **risk scores** and **observation** pathways—pick the answer that matches the stem’s age and appearance.

lessons.title: Adolescent depression and safety: screening within scope (FNP)
lessons.seoTitle: FNP adolescent mental health screening | NurseNest
lessons.seoDescription: Family NP: PHQ-A, safety assessment, confidentiality limits, and referral—board-style adolescent primary care.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.body: FNP primary care must **screen** where evidence supports it, then **risk-stratify** suicidality—documentation and follow-up interval are part of the correct answer.

sections.heading: Core concepts
sections.body: Validated tools (e.g., PHQ-9/PHQ-A) **quantify severity**; they do not replace a **direct safety inquiry** when the stem gives concerning cues.

sections.heading: Clinical application
sections.body: Positive screen + passive suicidal ideation without plan: **safety plan**, caregiver involvement when appropriate, **urgent psychiatry/ crisis** referral per acuity, and **short-interval follow-up** in clinic.

sections.heading: Exam tips
sections.body: Items pit **confidentiality** against **imminent harm**—choose the pathway that protects life while respecting adolescent rights where the stem allows.

lessons.title: Adult hypertension: intensification and follow-up (FNP)
lessons.seoDescription: Family NP: JNC-style thresholds, comorbidity-driven targets, combo therapy, labs after ACE/ARB, and follow-up intervals for boards.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.body: Hypertension items test **who** gets **how many** agents, **which class**, and **when** to recheck labs—not memorizing a single universal goal.

sections.heading: Core concepts
sections.body: CKD, diabetes, and ASCVD shift **targets** and **preferred classes**; pregnancy and bilateral renal artery stenosis change what is safe.

sections.heading: Clinical application
sections.body: Uncontrolled Stage 2 on one agent: **add complementary class** (e.g., ACE/ARB + CCB/thiazide) rather than maxing a single drug without rationale; schedule **2–4 week** follow-up after change.

sections.heading: Exam tips
sections.body: Eliminate options that **skip baseline labs** when starting ACE/ARB or that **defer therapy** when the stem shows sustained severe elevation without contraindication.

lessons.title: Geriatric falls and syncope: medication and rhythm (FNP)
lessons.seoDescription: Family NP: orthostasis, polypharmacy, rhythm risk, bone injury, and coordinated fall prevention for certification-level reasoning.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.body: Geriatric falls are **multifactorial**; boards reward **medication review**, **orthostatic vitals**, and **ECG** when syncope is unexplained—not a single imaging order by default.

sections.heading: Core concepts
sections.body: Deprescribe **sedatives**, **hypnotics**, and **anticholinergics** when possible; address **vision**, **footwear**, and **home hazards** alongside medical causes.

sections.heading: Clinical application
sections.body: Witnessed arrhythmia or exertional syncope: **ED/cardiology** pathway; mechanical fall with hip pain: **imaging**; recurrent near-falls with polypharmacy: **med reconciliation** and **PT referral**.

sections.heading: Exam tips
sections.body: Avoid picking **head CT** when the stem lacks neuro deficit and points to **perfusion/medication** causes unless guidelines in the item support it.
